summaries.one_sentence: Keshav's 2007 essay proposes a three-pass method for reading research papers — bird's-eye, content, and reconstruction — that turns a pile of PDFs into a reproducible habit and a personal literature review.
three_sentence: First read the title, abstract, intro, section headings, conclusion and references in five to ten minutes to classify the paper and decide whether to read deeper.
Second read the body with care, skipping proofs, to understand the main ideas, claims and figures, ideally within an hour.
Third read the paper as if you were its author — virtualising the system, identifying implicit assumptions, criticising the evidence — to internalise the work and to be able to reproduce it.
ten_sentence: Keshav opens by observing that reading a paper is a different activity at five minutes, one hour, and four hours, and proposes the three-pass method as a way to honour that difference deliberately.
The first pass is a 5–10 minute bird's-eye read that answers five questions called the Five Cs: Category, Context, Correctness, Contributions, Clarity.
The first pass is explicitly a triage step: its output is a decision to continue to Pass 2, to seek references first, or to stop.
Pass 2 is a roughly one-hour deeper read that focuses on the main ideas, claims, figures and tables, deliberately skipping proofs.
Pass 3 is a 1–4 hour reconstructive read in which the reader plays the role of the author, virtualises the system, identifies hidden assumptions, and writes a critique.
The method is designed for graduate students but applies to anyone who reads papers regularly and wants to convert reading into understanding.
Keshav frames the cost–benefit calculus: a first pass costs little and is high-value; a third pass is only worth it for foundational papers.
He recommends keeping a simple logbook of papers read with one-sentence summaries, which compounds into a literature review over time.
The essay is short, opinionated, and deliberately minimal — its value is the workflow, not the framework's completeness.
Its weakness is that it does not address modern reading aids (LLMs, OCR, semantic search) and assumes a single-author reader; a 2026 update would need to extend it.

five_cs.category: Methods / how-to essay in computer science research methodology — not original research, but a meta-paper proposing a paper-reading workflow.
five_cs.context: Builds on a long tradition of advice-for-grad-students essays in SIGCOMM and adjacent communities; complementary to Bird & Wadge's Introduction to Programming, Hennessy & Patterson's architecture textbooks, and earlier 'how to do research' guides from the 1980s and 1990s.
five_cs.correctness: The five-question framework is plausible and self-consistent; the time estimates (5–10 min, ~1 h, 1–4 h) are reasonable for the assumed reader (CS graduate student, English-speaking) but not validated empirically. [Author claim].
contributions: A three-pass reading procedure with explicit per-pass goals and time budgets.
The Five Cs checklist (Category, Context, Correctness, Contributions, Clarity) as a portable Pass-1 artifact.
A logbook discipline (one-sentence summaries per paper) that converts ad-hoc reading into a personal literature review over time.
five_cs.clarity: Exceptionally clear. The essay is three pages, plain prose, no jargon, with explicit advice for each pass. It is a model of the genre.

pass1.bird_eye_notes: Title, abstract, intro and headings signal a three-pass reading method. No figures. No equations. Six short references. The whole paper is roughly three pages of SIGCOMM CCR — it reads more like an op-ed than a research article.
pass1.decision_rationale: Even though the paper is short, it is widely cited and foundational in the genre; Pass 3 (reconstruction) is warranted to extract a personal workflow.

main_ideas: Reading a paper is a different activity at 5 min, 1 h, and 4 h; design for that.
Pass 1 is a bird's-eye triage that produces a continue-or-stop decision, not a summary.
Pass 1's deliverable is the Five Cs — five short answers, not a paragraph of prose.
Pass 2 reads the body with care but skips proofs; its goal is to understand the main ideas, not to verify them.
Pass 3 reconstructs the paper as if the reader were the author; the artifact is a critique and a virtualised system, not a summary.
A logbook of one-sentence summaries compounds into a literature review over months and years.

key_references.title: Introduction to Programming
key_references.why: cited as exemplar of Pass-2 reading: read code as well as prose

key_references.title: Computer Architecture: A Quantitative Approach
key_references.why: cited as a book that benefits from Pass-3 reconstruction

key_references.title: How to Do Research (AIC essay)
key_references.why: earlier essay in the same advice-for-grad-students genre

key_references.title: The Craft of Research
key_references.why: broader research-method book that the logbook habit complements

method_reconstruction: Define three time-budgeted passes: Pass 1 = 5–10 min, Pass 2 = ~1 h, Pass 3 = 1–4 h. The budgets are non-negotiable.
Pass 1 reads in this order: title, abstract, intro, section headings, conclusion, references, and glances at figures and math. No deep reading.
Pass 1 produces the Five Cs: Category, Context, Correctness, Contributions, Clarity. Each is one to three sentences.
Pass 1's terminal artifact is a continue-or-stop decision (CONTINUE_FULL / CONTINUE_PARTIAL / SEEK_REFERENCES_FIRST / STOP).
Pass 2 reads the body in detail, but skips proofs. Its artifacts are main ideas (3–7), figures/tables (one paragraph each), and key references (5–15).
Pass 2's load-bearing artifact is a Claims → Evidence map: every main claim paired with the figure/table/section that grounds it.
Pass 3 reads the paper as if the reader were its author: virtualise the system, name hidden assumptions, find the failure modes, write a critique.
Pass 3's terminal artifact is a reproduction plan: dataset, baseline, hardware, steps, sanity checks, success criteria.
Maintain a logbook. Every paper read gets a one-sentence summary plus its Five Cs. Review monthly; promote survivors to a literature-review draft.
Repeat. The skill is a habit, not a one-off. After 50–100 papers the logbook becomes the first draft of a survey.
critical_review: Time budgets (5–10 min / ~1 h / 1–4 h) are asserted, not measured. A modern reader with semantic-search tooling will probably spend less time on Pass 1, more on Pass 2.
The method assumes the reader is a CS graduate student reading in English. It does not adapt to interdisciplinary reading, slow-language readers, or visually impaired readers.
The Five Cs are useful but incomplete: they do not include Reproducibility, Societal Impact, or Open Science as first-class Cs.
No mention of modern reading aids: LLMs, semantic search, citation graphs, or active reading tools. A 2026 update should integrate these explicitly.
The logbook habit is good advice but the essay does not say how to keep the logbook (Markdown vs spreadsheet vs notebook) or how to handle multi-author papers.
The Pass 3 reconstruction is the most valuable and least taught part of the method; the essay under-explains how to virtualise a system.

claims_evidence_map.claim_text: Reading a paper is a different activity at 5 min, 1 h, and 4 h.
claims_evidence_map.notes: Foundational framing for the three-pass structure.

claims_evidence_map.claim_text: Pass 1 takes 5–10 minutes and produces the Five Cs.
claims_evidence_map.notes: Direct quote of the method's first-pass spec.

claims_evidence_map.claim_text: Pass 1's output is a continue-or-stop decision, not a summary.
Pass 3 should virtualise the system the paper describes.
A logbook of one-sentence summaries compounds into a literature review.
claims_evidence_map.notes: Plausible and widely repeated, but no empirical evidence cited.

claims_evidence_map.claim_text: The Five Cs are: Category, Context, Correctness, Contributions, Clarity.
The method is for graduate students and researchers in computer science.
claims_evidence_map.notes: Scope claim; agent-inferred applicability to other fields.

claims_evidence_map.claim_text: Three pages of SIGCOMM CCR is the right length for a how-to essay.
claims_evidence_map.notes: Format judgement, not in the paper.

claims_evidence_map.claim_text: The method is widely cited and influential in CS pedagogy.

glossary.definition: A reading procedure that splits one paper into three time-budgeted reads: bird's-eye, content, reconstruction.
Pass 1's five-question checklist: Category, Context, Correctness, Contributions, Clarity.
A running personal record of papers read, with one-sentence summary and Five Cs per entry.
In Pass 3, mentally re-implement the paper's system from scratch as if you were the author.
A paper whose ideas you must internalise before reading the rest of its sub-field; warrants Pass 3.
Pass 1's terminal decision: continue (Pass 2/3), seek references first, or stop.

limitations: Time budgets are asserted, not measured.
Scope is CS graduate students in English; interdisciplinary reading is unaddressed.
Five Cs do not include Reproducibility, Societal Impact, or Open Science.
No integration with modern reading aids (LLMs, semantic search, citation graphs).
Logbook advice is good but the essay does not say how to keep the logbook.
Pass 3 is the most valuable and least taught step; virtualisation is under-explained.

reproduction_plan.dataset: n/a — method paper. To reproduce the *experience* of the method, the dataset is the set of 50 papers the reader chooses to read in a semester.
reproduction_plan.baseline: Read-all-papers-linearly with no time budget and no logbook.
reproduction_plan.hardware: A laptop and a Markdown editor. No GPU, no cloud.
steps: Read 10 papers using the three-pass method and write a logbook entry per paper.
Read 10 papers linearly (no time budget, no logbook) as a control.
After two weeks, score each set on: comprehension (self-test), recall (one-month delayed), ability to summarise in one sentence.
Compare the two distributions; expect the three-pass set to outperform on comprehension and recall.
sanity_checks: Each Pass 1 finishes within 10 minutes (timer on).
Each logbook entry has a one-sentence summary and the Five Cs filled in.
Pass 3 includes a virtualisation step, not just a critique.
success_criteria: 50 papers read, 50 logbook entries, all with Five Cs.
One-month-delayed recall ≥ 70% on a self-test for the three-pass cohort.
A literature-review draft can be assembled from the logbook in ≤ 1 day.

open_questions: How should the three-pass method adapt when LLMs can summarise or extract claims automatically?
How does the method scale to interdisciplinary reading where the Five Cs may not transfer cleanly?
Is the logbook discipline still the right meta-habit in a world of citation graphs and semantic search?
How do you teach Pass 3 to a reader who has never implemented a system?
Should Reproducibility be promoted from a hidden concern to a sixth C?

final_checklist.question: Can I state the paper's main contribution in one sentence?
Can I name the three passes and their time budgets?
Can I name the Five Cs without looking?
Can I describe what 'virtualise a system' means in Pass 3?
Can I explain why a logbook compounds into a literature review?
Can I name two limitations of the method?
Can I describe a small experiment that would test whether the method works?
Can I name two ways the method would change if it were rewritten in 2026?
Have I marked every interpretive statement with an evidence label?
Have I honestly recorded the reading mode as full_text?

paper_outline.title: 2. The first pass
subsections: The Five Cs (Category, Context, Correctness, Contributions, Clarity)

steps: Input kind = complete_paper (essay, 3 pages, SIGCOMM CCR).
Title and author identified directly from the essay's header.
Year (2007) and venue (SIGCOMM Computer Communication Review) confirmed from the essay's masthead.
No DOI / arXiv / OpenReview ID — method paper from a community magazine.
Reading mode = full_text (the whole essay was read).
